title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Deciphering the Wild: Can Animals Talk?
content.text: The idea that humans are the only species capable of complex communication has long been a cornerstone of our self-identity. We defined ourselves by our syntax, our abstract concepts, and our ability to tell stories. However, recent breakthroughs in biology and machine learning are beginning to challenge this linguistic exceptionalism. || (41) || [41] For decades, researchers tried to teach apes human sign language, with limited success [/41]. While animals like Koko the gorilla could learn hundreds of signs, they rarely demonstrated the ability to use complex grammar. Critics argued that they were simply mimicking their trainers to get rewards, rather than truly 'talking.' || (42) || [42] The focus has now shifted from teaching animals 'human,' to understanding animals in their own 'tongues' [/42]. By using advanced algorithms to analyze thousands of hours of recordings, scientists are discovering that many species have vocalizations far more complex than we ever imagined. || (43) || [43] One of the most striking examples comes from the world of prairie dogs [/43]. These small rodents use a series of high-pitched chirps to alert their colony to predators. But these aren't just simple alarms. Analysis shows they can describe the size, color, and species of the threat—even distinguishing between a human in a blue t-shirt and one in yellow. || (44) || [44] In the ocean, the complexity is even greater [/44]. Sperm whales communicate using rhythmic patterns of clicks known as 'codas.' Recent studies suggest that these codas act like cultural identifiers, similar to human dialects, allowing whales to recognize members of their own clan across vast distances. || (45) || [45] This research is now being supercharged by the 'Interspecies Internet' project [/45]. By using AI to look for patterns in the massive datasets of whale, elephant, and crow communications, researchers hope to eventually create a 'Google Translate' for the animal kingdom. || (46) || [46] But even if we decode the sounds, will we understand the meaning? [/46] Language is deeply tied to the sensory experience of the speaker. A bat’s language would be based on echolocation; a dolphin’s on pressure and sound. Our challenge may not be in the translation of words, but in the translation of perspectives that are fundamentally alien to our own.

paragraphs.A: This suggests that the 'vocabulary' of even simple mammals is much larger than previously thought. If a rodent can communicate specific colors, what might a more social animal like a wolf or an elephant be capable of saying?
paragraphs.B: [43] Researchers found that by changing the frequency and duration of their barks, they could convey a remarkable amount of detail [/43]. It appears they are communicating a descriptive 'image' of the intruder to the rest of the group.
paragraphs.C: [41] These early experiments were often criticized for their anthropocentric bias [/41]. We were so obsessed with whether animals could speak like us that we failed to listen to how they were already speaking to each other.
paragraphs.D: [46] This raises profound philosophical questions about the nature of intelligence [/46]. If an animal can think and communicate, do we have a moral obligation to treat them as 'persons' with their own set of rights?
paragraphs.E: [42] This new approach relies on 'passive' observation rather than 'active' training [/42]. By recording animals in the wild without human interference, we are finally getting a glimpse into their private conversations.
paragraphs.F: [45] The goal is to identify the 'building blocks' of these non-human languages [/45]. If AI can find a structure similar to our own grammar in the clicks of a whale, it would suggest that the rules of communication are universal.
paragraphs.G: [44] Marine biologists have long known that cetaceans are highly social, but the depth of their vocal 'culture' is only now coming to light [/44]. Each group seems to have its own unique 'signature,' passed down through generations.

questions.explanation: C criticizes ancient experiments for their anthropocentric bias, connecting with the failed attempt to teach sign language to apes.
E describes the new 'passive observation' approach, which is the change in approach mentioned in the main text.
B provides the technical detail of how prairie dogs change the frequency of their barking to provide details.
G introduces the 'vocal culture' of cetaceans, which fits with the mention of sperm whales and their codas.
F explains the objective of using AI in this project: to find the 'building blocks' of animal language.
D raises the philosophical question about intelligence and rights, which follows the debate about whether we will understand the 'meaning' of what they say.
